name: relations
context: Define application-level relations in Drizzle using the relations() function for nested relational queries
info: Drizzle relations are application-level abstractions defined with the `relations()` function from `drizzle-orm`. They are separate from database foreign keys — relations don't create constraints, and foreign keys don't create relations. You define them by calling `relations(table, callback)` where the callback receives `one()` and `many()` helpers. Relations enable the relational query API (`db.query.*.findMany/findFirst` with `with` clauses) which loads nested data without manual JOINs. One-to-one uses `one()` with `fields` and `references`. One-to-many uses `many()` on the parent and `one()` on the child. Many-to-many requires an explicit junction table with `many()` on both sides and `one()` on the junction. Use `relationName` to disambiguate multiple relations between the same tables. Relations must be exported alongside tables for drizzle to discover them. See [[drizzle/schema]] for table definitions and [[drizzle/queries]] for querying with relations.
triggers: create a one-to-many relation in drizzle

sections.title: One-to-many relations
sections.body: Define `many(childTable)` on the parent's relations and `one(parentTable, { fields: [fk], references: [pk] })` on the child's relations. The `fields` array contains the foreign key column(s) on the current table; `references` contains the corresponding primary key column(s) on the related table. Both sides must be defined.

sections.title: One-to-one relations
sections.body: Same as one-to-many but with a unique constraint on the foreign key column. The side holding the FK defines `one(table, { fields, references })`. The other side defines `one(table)` without fields — the result is nullable. Ensure the FK column has `.unique()` in [[drizzle/schema]] to enforce the one-to-one constraint at the database level.

sections.title: Many-to-many relations
sections.body: Create an explicit junction table with foreign keys to both sides. Define `many(junctionTable)` on both parent tables. Define `one(parentA, { fields, references })` and `one(parentB, { fields, references })` on the junction table's relations. Query through the junction with nested `with` clauses. Drizzle never creates implicit join tables.

sections.title: Disambiguating with relationName
sections.body: When table A has multiple relations to table B (e.g., author and reviewer on posts), add `relationName: 'unique-name'` to both sides to match them. Without this, Drizzle cannot determine which FK maps to which relation. Use descriptive names like 'author', 'reviewer', 'parent-child'.

sections.title: Relations vs foreign keys
sections.body: Foreign keys (`.references()`) are database-level constraints enforced by the database engine. Relations (`relations()`) are application-level abstractions used only by Drizzle's relational query API. You can have one without the other. Best practice: define both — foreign keys for data integrity, relations for developer ergonomics and the `with` query syntax.

related.note: Tables and foreign key columns must be defined before relations
The relational query API (db.query.*.findMany with: {}) requires relations to be defined
Prisma defines relations in the schema language; Drizzle uses separate TypeScript definitions
